explicit_text: I was born in Burma, but we had to leave because my husband had problems with the government. The government was unfair and cared only for themselves, not the people. Leaving Burma was difficult, but it was necessary for our safety.
implicit_text: I was born in the Southeast Asian country ruled by a military government, but we had to leave because my husband had problems with the authorities. The regime was unfair and cared only for themselves, not the people. Leaving that country was difficult, but it was necessary for our safety.

explicit_text: After leaving Burma, my family and I lived in the Nu Poe Refugee Camp in Thailand for seven years. Life there was very hard; we didn’t have enough food and couldn’t go anywhere freely. During that time, my father passed away, and I never got to see him again, which makes me very sad.

explicit_text: We stayed in Thailand at the Nu Poe Refugee Camp after fleeing Burma. Thailand was a temporary place for us, but life was challenging and uncertain. It was there that I experienced many hardships before we were resettled to the United States.
implicit_text: We stayed in the Southeast Asian kingdom at the refugee camp after fleeing my home country. This nation was a temporary place for us, but life was challenging and uncertain. It was there that I experienced many hardships before we were resettled to the United States.

explicit_text: My family and I were resettled to the United States in 2013 after living in the refugee camp. Since arriving, I have been living in Minnesota and trying to improve my English. Although I face health challenges and financial worries, especially about my daughters’ education, I am grateful for the opportunities here.

explicit_text: I have been living in St. Paul, Minnesota for about one year and eight months with my husband and two daughters. This city is now our home, and my daughters are students here. I attend classes at the Ronald M. Hubbs Center to improve my English while managing my health and family responsibilities.
